tldr: Bucket is a hosted feature-flag SaaS; FF4J is a self-hosted open-source Java feature-toggle library. Migration means moving flag definitions out of Bucket's UI/API and recreating them in FF4J's chosen storage backend, then swapping SDK calls in code.
why_switch: Move from SaaS subscription to free open-source library
Self-host flag state in your existing database
Use the Spring Boot starter, web console, and audit trail
Reduce vendor lock-in for a Java/JVM stack
Choose from 20+ supported backends for storage
what_to_export: List of all flags with names, descriptions, and current state
Audit history of recent changes for compliance
API keys, webhooks, and integrations to replace
Code references to Bucket SDK calls per service
export_method: Export flags from Bucket via whatever administrative export is available (UI export, API listing, or CLI). Record per flag: name, description, default value, type (boolean, string, number), targeting rules, percentage rollouts, and per-environment state. If Bucket exposes an audit log, snapshot it for compliance archives. Map Bucket's user/segment model into attributes you can re-create in FF4J's property model. Inventory every code path that calls the Bucket SDK — class, method, flag name, default value — so you can plan the SDK swap. If a direct export endpoint is not available, paginate through their API and serialize results to a JSON or YAML file you control. Keep the Bucket account active in read-only mode until FF4J is fully cut over so you have a fallback for definitions and audit data.
import_method: Stand up FF4J in your Java service: add the ff4j-core dependency (and ff4j-spring-boot-starter if on Spring Boot) and pick a storage backend from the 20+ supported options (relational DB, Redis, Mongo, etc.). Configure the FF4J store and load flags either via the bundled XML/YAML configuration or through the FF4J admin REST API by replaying your exported list. Recreate user/property providers so targeting works equivalently. Replace Bucket SDK calls with FF4J's check API (FF4j.check("flag-name") or FeatureToggle annotations on Spring beans). Stand up the FF4J web console for non-engineering changes and configure RBAC. Run both systems in parallel for one release cycle, comparing flag evaluation outcomes, then cut over by removing the Bucket SDK and decommissioning the account.
gotchas: FF4J is Java-only — any non-JVM service currently calling Bucket needs a different solution
Targeting rules in Bucket may not map cleanly to FF4J flipping strategies; complex rules become custom Java strategy classes
Self-hosting shifts ops burden: you now own uptime, backups, and schema migrations for the flag store
FF4J 2.0.0 was released in early 2023; release cadence is slower than SaaS competitors — verify current activity before committing
Audit history from Bucket likely cannot be ingested into FF4J's audit trail; archive separately for compliance
Run both systems in parallel for at least one release to catch evaluation mismatches before cutover

email.subject: Migrating feature flags from Bucket to self-hosted FF4J
email.body:
Hi team,

We are planning a 14-day migration from Bucket (hosted feature flag SaaS) to FF4J, the open-source Java feature toggle library, for our JVM services. The drivers are eliminating the SaaS subscription, self-hosting flag state in our existing database, and reducing vendor dependencies.

The plan: export all flags and targeting rules from Bucket, stand up FF4J with a chosen backend (likely our existing relational DB), recreate flags via the admin REST API, swap SDK calls module-by-module, and run both systems in parallel for one release before cutover. The FF4J web console will replace the Bucket UI for non-engineering changes.

A call-out: FF4J is Java-only. If you own any non-JVM service that currently uses Bucket, please raise it now so we can plan a separate path.

Thanks,
Your customer
